title: Mathematics
subtitle: Week 3: Parallel, Intersecting, and Perpendicular Lines

slides.title: Lines and Rulers Slide 1
slides.text: Parallel lines are straight lines that are always the same distance apart and never cross or touch each other, no matter how far they are extended. We see parallel lines in many daily objects. They are important because they help keep structures straight and balanced. The rows of pineapples planted in the fields of Polomolok run parallel to each other, stretching out towards the base of Mount Matutum. Where else can we see parallel lines in our school classrooms or along local roads? Let us look. Card 1. To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation.

examples.title: Example 1
examples.content: Parallel lines: ||. Perpendicular lines form a 90-degree angle (T-shape). Equal segments: two lines both 5 cm long.

slides.title: Lines and Rulers Slide 9
slides.text: Intersecting lines are lines that cross each other at a single point. The point where they cross is called the point of intersection. When two lines intersect, they form angles. Intersecting lines show paths that meet or cross in space. In the city center of Koronadal, major streets cross each other, creating busy intersecting roads where traffic lights control the flow. How many crossing points do intersecting lines have, and what happens at that point? Study slide 9. To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation.

examples.title: Example 9
examples.content: Parallel lines: ||. Perpendicular lines form a 90-degree angle (T-shape). Equal segments: two lines both 5 cm long.

slides.title: Lines and Rulers Slide 17
slides.text: Perpendicular lines are a special type of intersecting lines that cross each other at exactly right angles, forming four square corners. A right angle is an angle of ninety degrees, like the corner of a square tile or paper sheet. The steel columns of the giant fish processing ports in General Santos City stand perpendicular to the flat concrete docks. How can we identify if two intersecting lines are perpendicular by looking at their corners? Card 17. To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation.

slides.title: Lines and Rulers Slide 23
slides.text: We can draw line segments of equal length using a ruler by carefully marking the start at zero and the endpoint at the desired number. Keeping your pencil sharp and your ruler steady ensures that the line segments are drawn accurately. Art students in Lake Sebu use rulers to draw equal line segments when designing geometric patterns on T'nalak fabrics. Why is it important to start measuring from the zero mark on a ruler when drawing equal line segments? Slide 23. To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation.

examples.title: Example 23

challenge.q: Line A is parallel to Line B. Line B is parallel to Line C. What is the relationship between Line A and Line C?
options: They are parallel to each other

options: They are parallel to each other

pages:
Exercise 1: Identifying Types of Lines

Write Parallel, Intersecting, or Perpendicular for each pair of lines below.

1. Lines that cross and form four square corners:
Perpendicular
2. Lines that run side-by-side and never cross:
Parallel

Exercise 2: Drawing Equal Line Segments

Answer the questions about ruler measurements and line segment lengths.

1. To draw a line segment of exactly 8 cm, where should you place the start point on the ruler?
0 mark

Exercise 3: Geometry in Art

Identify geometric line patterns found in local South Cotabato arts.

1. What type of line patterns are formed by the crossing warp and weft fibers in T'nalak weaving?
Intersecting

performance.title: Street Map Designer with Lines
performance.desc: On a clean sheet of paper, design a simple road map for a barangay in Polomolok. Draw at least two parallel roads (label them Pineapple Road and Matutum Road). Draw another road that intersects both parallel roads (label it Mountain Road). Finally, draw a road that is perpendicular to Pineapple Road (label it Market Road). Draw two equal-sized houses using your ruler. Take a photo of your labeled map and upload it here.
labels: Correct parallel and perpendicular roads drawing
Correct drawing of two equal-sized houses using a ruler
